message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 11
Recent Green phase: Added moonstone: 25 to BASE_PREMIUMS map. Existing rune-vs-non-rune branching still handles moonstones since they're not runes (no block applied to mixed types).

Note: The next test in the list is "3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)" — meaning both rune AND moonstone groups can form blocks. The current code only special-cases runes. Don't pre-implement, but consider whether the rune-vs-non-rune split is the right shape.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: All 11 tests pass. Now let me run ESLint for PRE-measurement.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -100

content.text:
**PRE-Measurement: ESLint smells = 0, no cognitive complexity warnings.**

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for `quotePremium` (the most complex function):

**PRE-Measurement: APP mass for `quotePremium`**

Walking the function body:
```typescript
const quotePremium = (items: Item[]): number => {
  const runeCount = items.filter((i) => i.type === "rune").length;
  const nonRuneBase = items
    .filter((i) => i.type !== "rune")
    .reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type], 0);
  const runeBase =
    runeCount === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE_G : runeCount * RUNE_UNIT_PRICE_G;
  const policyBase = nonRuneBase + runeBase;
  const withFirstInsuranceSurcharge = policyBase + policyBase / FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_DIVISOR;
  return Math.ceil(withFirstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE_G);
};
```

Counting:
- Constants: 3 ("rune", "rune", 0) = 3
- Bindings: `items`, `i`(twice), `sum`, `item`, `runeCount`, `nonRuneBase`, `runeBase`, `policyBase`, `withFirstInsuranceSurcharge`, `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE_G`, `RUNE_UNIT_PRICE_G`, `BASE_PREMIUMS`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_DIVISOR`, `PROCESSING_FEE_G` ≈ 16
- Invocations: `filter`, `.length`(prop access, not counted), `filter`, `.reduce`, `Math.ceil` = ~5 invocations (filter ×2, reduce ×1, ceil ×1, plus arithmetic). Let's count function calls strictly: filter, filter, reduce, Math.ceil = 4. Arithmetic operators (+, /, *, ===, !==) act as invocations in APP loosely: ===, !==, +, +, +, +, /, *, + = 9 more. So total ~13.
- Conditionals: 1 (the ternary `runeCount === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE ? ... : ...`)
- Loops: 2 (filter, filter, reduce iterates too) → filter×2 + reduce = 3 loops
- Assignments: 0 (only `const`, no mutation)

Mass ≈ 3·1 + 16·1 + 13·2 + 1·4 + 3·5 + 0·6 = 3 + 16 + 26 + 4 + 15 = **64**

**PRE-Measurement: McCabe for `quotePremium`**
- Start: 1
- Ternary `runeCount === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE ? ... : ...`: +1
- McCabe: **2**

**PRE-Measurement: McCabe for `processScenario`**
- Start: 1
- No branches
- McCabe: **1**

**Naming Evaluation:**
- `quotePremium`: clearly reveals intent (compute premium for a quote). Keep.
- `processScenario`: reveals intent. Keep.
- The variable `nonRuneBase` and the rune-vs-non-rune split is now smelly given the upcoming test makes moonstones also blockable. This is structural: not naming.

**Refactoring Target Decision:**

McCabe is only 2 — already low. ESLint smells: 0. But the **structure** has a problem: the rune-special-case will need to generalize to any "blockable type" (moonstones next). The hint in the prompt says: "consider whether the rune-vs-non-rune split is the right shape."

Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) and Rule 3 (No Duplication): The duplication isn't visible yet, but the asymmetry between "rune" and "everything else" hardcodes a single-type assumption. Refactoring this now (while only one type uses blocks) is cheap. But — measurement discipline says: refactor based on what the metrics show, and don't over-engineer for tests that haven't been written yet.

Let me consider what refactoring would simplify the CURRENT code WITHOUT pre-implementing future tests.

Looking at the current code, the `nonRuneBase` calculation uses `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` for moonstones too (since `moonstone` is in BASE_PREMIUMS at 25). The `2 runes + 1 moonstone` test confirms this works: 2×25 + 1×25 = 75, no block.

One concrete improvement: compute base premiums per type via grouping, so the same calculation handles rune-like block logic for any type. But that would be pre-implementation.

A simpler, naming-only improvement: `runeCount === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` could be clearer. Let me look at what makes the structure less rune-centric without changing behavior. 

Actually, a clean naming improvement: extract `runeBlockPrice` as a helper function. This isolates the block-pricing logic and improves Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) without speculating about generalization.

**Refactoring choice:** Extract a small helper `priceForRunes(count)` to express the rune-block pricing rule independently. This makes the intent clearer and creates a seam for future generalization without pre-implementing it.

Let me check: does this actually reduce any metric? Extract function adds invocation cost. McCabe of caller would drop from 2 to 1 (no more ternary). McCabe of new helper: 2. Total McCabe across functions: still 2 vs 2. Mass: extract adds invocation (+2) but removes ternary (-4) from caller. Net for caller: lower.

Actually let me reconsider. Looking again: the metrics are already excellent. The CURRENT code has McCabe 2, cognitive ~0, ESLint smells 0. Any change must demonstrably help.

Let me consider an alternative refactor that helps now AND prepares structure: replace the rune-specific block logic with a `groupBy`-then-`priceGroup` pattern. But this is pre-implementation.

Let me consider an even smaller, clearer change. The `2 runes + 1 moonstone` test passes because moonstones are picked up by `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` in the `nonRuneBase` filter. But "nonRuneBase" is now a misleading name — it really contains moonstones too. **The name no longer reveals intent.** 

Rename `nonRuneBase` → `unblockedItemsBase` (items that don't form a rune block). This is a Rule 2 fix driven by the new test.

Actually wait — moonstones ARE non-runes, so the name is technically still correct. But the semantic is "items not in the rune-block". Let me think harder...

Let me look more carefully. The cleanest refactor: extract the rune-counting/pricing into a named helper that captures intent.

content.text: All 11 tests still pass. Now POST-measurements.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -100

content.text:
No tasks needed for this single-cycle refactor — proceeding with measurements.

**POST-Measurement: APP mass for `quotePremium`**
```typescript
const quotePremium = (items: Item[]): number => {
  const runeCount = items.filter((i) => i.type === "rune").length;
  const otherItemsBase = items
    .filter((i) => i.type !== "rune")
    .reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type], 0);
  const policyBase = otherItemsBase + priceForRunes(runeCount);
  const withFirstInsuranceSurcharge = policyBase + policyBase / FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_DIVISOR;
  return Math.ceil(withFirstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE_G);
};
```

- Constants: 2 ("rune", 0) = 2
- Bindings: items, i, i, sum, item, runeCount, otherItemsBase, policyBase, withFirstInsuranceSurcharge, BASE_PREMIUMS, FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_DIVISOR, PROCESSING_FEE_G ≈ 13
- Invocations: filter, filter, reduce, priceForRunes, Math.ceil = 5 strict calls; plus operators (===, !==, +, +, +, /, +) ≈ 7. Total ~12.
- Conditionals: 0 (ternary moved out)
- Loops: 3 (filter×2, reduce)
- Assignments: 0

Mass ≈ 2 + 13 + 24 + 0 + 15 + 0 = **54**

**POST-Measurement: APP mass for `priceForRunes`**
```typescript
const priceForRunes = (count: number): number =>
  count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE_G : count * BASE_PREMIUMS.rune;
```
- Constants: 1 ("rune" via property access — count it as a binding access)
- Bindings: count, RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE, RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE_G, BASE_PREMIUMS = 4
- Invocations: ===, * = 2
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary)
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Mass ≈ 0 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 0 + 0 = **12**

**POST-Measurement: McCabe**
- `quotePremium`: start 1, no branches → **1** (was 2)
- `priceForRunes`: start 1, ternary +1 → **2**

**Summary of deltas:**

| Metric | PRE | POST |
|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells (total) | 0 | 0 |
| Cognitive complexity warnings | 0 | 0 |
| `quotePremium` APP mass | ~64 | ~54 |
| `quotePremium` McCabe | 2 | 1 |
| `priceForRunes` (new) APP mass | n/a | 12 |
| `priceForRunes` (new) McCabe | n/a | 2 |
| Tests | 11/11 passing | 11/11 passing |

**Decision: Keep the refactoring.**
- McCabe of the main function dropped from 2 to 1
- Mass of the main function dropped (~64 → ~54)
- The rune-block-pricing rule is now isolated in `priceForRunes` — a named seam ready to be generalized or duplicated for moonstones when the next test arrives
- I also removed the redundant `RUNE_UNIT_PRICE_G` constant (it duplicated `BASE_PREMIUMS.rune = 25`), eliminating a Rule 3 duplication

**Naming evaluation:**
- Renamed `nonRuneBase` → `otherItemsBase` because the variable now contains moonstones (non-runes priced individually). The new name reveals intent — items not consolidated into the rune block — without misleadingly implying "everything but runes is special".
- New helper `priceForRunes` is purpose-named for the block-pricing rule.

---

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `priceForRunes(count)` helper; removed duplicate `RUNE_UNIT_PRICE_G` constant (used `BASE_PREMIUMS.rune` instead); renamed `nonRuneBase` to `otherItemsBase` for accuracy now that moonstones are in the mix.
- **Smell delta**: 0 → 0
- **Cognitive delta** (`quotePremium`): negligible → negligible
- **APP mass delta** (`quotePremium`): ~64 → ~54
- **McCabe delta** (`quotePremium`): 2 → 1 (new helper `priceForRunes` McCabe 2, mass 12)
- **Tests**: All 11 passing

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_14-28-32_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts`
